steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The skill is Command of Evidence (Textual). The claim is that Aristophanes satirizes sophists as teaching students to be persuasive without regard for what is morally good. The correct quotation must show a sophist (or someone hiring one) explicitly prioritizing persuasion over moral goodness.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Socrates, a sophist, says that he studies astronomy while in a basket hanging a few feet off the ground because "I should not have rightly discovered things celestial if I had not suspended the intellect, and mixed the thought in a subtle form with its kindred air.": This quotation describes an unusual method of study, not instruction in winning arguments regardless of morality. It does not support the satirical claim. ✗
  • Choice B — Strepsiades encourages his son to learn to be a sophist, saying, "Go, I entreat you, dearest of men, go and be taught.": This shows a parent encouraging attendance, but the quotation contains no mention of immoral argument or disregard for what is good. ✗
  • Choice C — Socrates, a sophist, says to a new customer "Come now: what do you now wish to learn first of those things in none of which you have ever been instructed?": This is a generic intake question. It says nothing about teaching unethical persuasion, so it does not illustrate the satirical claim. ✗
  • Choice D — Strepsiades hires a sophist to teach his son and says to the sophist, "But see that he learns those two [styles of argument]; the better, whatever it may be; and the worse, which, by maintaining what is unjust, overturns the better. If not both, at any rate the unjust one by all means.": The quotation explicitly names “the worse” style that “by maintaining what is unjust, overturns the better,” and demands the sophist teach it “by all means.” This directly illustrates sophists teaching persuasion without regard for moral good. ✓
That is the correct answer.
